summary:
I. Failed Transaction Phenomena
The three transactions form a coordinated attack on the Audius DAO governance system. The core failure manifests in the successful manipulation of the governance process to drain funds from the DAO treasury. The attack transaction (0x4227bca8...9980dc9) successfully transferred 18,564,497.82 AUDIO tokens (worth approximately $6.8 million) from the DAO treasury contract (0x4deca517...3abac) to the attacker-controlled contract (0xbdbB5945...2e569). Despite this being classified as a "failed transaction" scenario, all three transactions executed successfully on-chain, indicating that the vulnerability lay in the protocol's logic rather than a technical execution failure.

II. Failed Transaction Core Operations
The attack was executed through three sequential transactions:

The first auxiliary transaction (0xfefd829e...877a984) deployed a malicious contract (0xbdbB5945...2e569) that was initialized with the ability to act as both a governance participant and a service provider. This contract was granted 10,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000 units of staking power (an astronomically large amount) through a delegateStake call in the same transaction, effectively giving it control over the governance voting process.

The second auxiliary transaction (0x3c09c630...dd5d5) used the deployed malicious contract to submit a vote (vote type 2, representing "support") on Proposal ID 85, exploiting the enormous staked voting power to ensure the proposal would pass.

The attack transaction (0x4227bca8...9980dc9) executed the maliciously passed Proposal ID 85, which triggered a transfer of 18,564,497.82 AUDIO tokens from the DAO treasury to the attacker-controlled contract.

The vulnerability stems from a critical flaw in the Audius governance system: the proposal submission mechanism allowed specifying arbitrary calldata for execution, and the voting power calculation failed to properly validate the source of staked tokens or prevent artificially inflated staking amounts. Specifically, the attacker deployed a contract that simultaneously acted as the governance contract, service provider factory, and token staker, allowing it to self-delegate an enormous amount of voting power without actually possessing or depositing real AUDIO tokens. The system's initialize functions accepted the attacker-controlled contract itself as the token address and governance address, creating a circular reference that bypassed proper token balance verification. Additionally, the delegateStake function did not verify that the staked amount corresponded to actual token transfers or existing balances, enabling the attacker to fabricate arbitrary voting weight.
